facts.text: Cassadaga’s name comes from a Seneca word meaning "water beneath the rocks," a nod to the spring‑fed lakes that define the village.
The village sits at the south end of Lower Cassadaga Lake, with Lily Dale on the east shore of Upper Cassadaga Lake and a narrow Middle Lake linking them.
In the 1870s, the Dunkirk, Allegheny Valley and Pittsburgh Railroad ran along the west side of the Cassadaga Lakes, bringing commerce to the area.
The village was formally incorporated in 1921, after settlers had moved westward following the forced removal of the Seneca and other Iroquois tribes after the Revolutionary War.
